steps.title: Track the shift in evidence
steps.content: The text first grants Papadakis et al.'s 2022 position: antigravity lacked affirmative experimental support, but evidence against it was also lacking, so theoretical consideration was reasonable. It then introduces the 2023 antihydrogen observations, which matched gravitational attraction and were thoroughly inconsistent with antigravity.

steps.title: Reject unsupported alternatives
steps.content: Choice A claims the CERN observations were conducted to test specific conclusions from the 2022 paper, but the text never states that purpose. Choice C invents an assumption that unambiguous proof would not be achievable. Choice D claims theoretical focus hindered experiments, while the text describes the 2022 reasoning as justified at the time.

steps.title: Choose the two-part summary
steps.content: The best main idea needs both halves: the earlier rationale made sense before contrary evidence existed, and the later report made that rationale no longer applicable. Choice B says exactly that the 2023 findings undercut the reason Papadakis et al. had offered for theoretical consideration. The answer is B.
